assemblyUnits: ELF note section `.note.Xen` carrying XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY = address of pvh_start (placed via linker script)
pvh_start (.code32): establish a tiny boot stack, save %ebx (hvm_start_info ptr)
long-mode trampoline (.code32->.code64): build boot PML4/PDPT/PDE identity map (2 MB pages), load CR3, set CR4.PAE, wrmsr EFER.LME (0xC000_0080), set CR0.PG|PE, lgdt 64-bit GDT, ljmp to 64-bit CS
rust handoff (.code64): set final SP, zero .bss, move saved hvm_start_info ptr into rdi, call rust_main
decisions.perArch: x86_64 ONLY. aarch64 has no equivalent mode switch (see aarch64 boot decision).

decisions.decision: On aarch64, ship a flat kernel `Image` with the 64-byte ARM64 boot header (magic 0x644d5241) and a single `_start` that assumes the ARM64 Linux boot contract: entered at EL1 with the MMU OFF, all interrupts masked in PSTATE.DAIF, and x0 = physical address of the device-tree blob (FDT) Firecracker generated. `_start` parses nothing in asm beyond saving x0, then jumps to safe Rust.
decisions.rationale: The ARM64 booting.rst contract is exact: primary CPU enters with x0=dtb phys addr, x1=x2=x3=0, MMU off, I-cache may be on, DAIF (Debug/SError/IRQ/FIQ) all masked, CPU in non-secure EL2 (recommended) or EL1; the decompressed Image carries a 64-byte header whose magic is 0x644d5241 ('ARM\x64'). Firecracker passes the FDT to the guest (its aarch64 machine is device-tree, not ACPI), so x0 must be threaded into the Rust FDT parser. Because the VMM hands us an MMU-off environment, turning the MMU on is our trampoline (unlike x86 where Firecracker can pre-page in LinuxBoot).
assemblyUnits: 64-byte Image header block (global_asm!): code0/code1 branch-to-_start, text_offset, image_size, flags, magic=0x644d5241
_start (.text): mask DAIF (defensive), save x0(FDT) to a static, set SP from reserved boot stack, zero .bss, bl rust_main
mmu_enable trampoline: program MAIR_EL1, TCR_EL1, TTBR0_EL1/TTBR1_EL1 (boot identity tables), then msr SCTLR_EL1 (set M) + isb
set VBAR_EL1 to the vector table (see trap decision)
decisions.perArch: aarch64 ONLY. Confirm entry EL at runtime (EL2 vs EL1) and, if EL2, drop to EL1 before MMU bring-up; Firecracker enters guest at EL1 so the drop path is normally inert.

decisions.decision: Implement `context_switch` to save/restore ONLY the ABI callee-saved set plus the return address/SP; rely on the soft-float/no-SIMD kernel target so NO floating-point or vector state is saved on context switch or trap.
decisions.rationale: Asterinas' switch.S/TaskContext stores exactly rsp, rbx, rbp, r12, r13, r14, r15 and rip — the System V AMD64 callee-saved set — using the fallthrough trick (context_switch saves cur, falls through into first_context_switch which restores nxt; the return address is moved, not pushed, for stack balance). AAPCS64 Table 2 defines the aarch64 callee-saved set as r19-r28, with r29=FP and r30=LR, plus SP. The `x86_64-unknown-none` target by default 'does not use any vector or floating-point registers (e.g. SSE, AVX)', so the kernel never dirties XMM/NEON state and the switch path stays tiny; the matching aarch64 soft-float target removes d8-d15 from the live set.
assemblyUnits: x86_64 switch.S: save/restore {rsp, rbx, rbp, r12, r13, r14, r15, rip}; context_switch + first_context_switch (fallthrough) + kernel_task_entry_wrapper (.cfi_undefined rip to terminate unwind)
aarch64 switch.S: save/restore {x19-x28, x29(fp), x30(lr), sp}; first-entry wrapper that loads the new task entry into the link path
TaskContext #[repr(C)] struct in Rust mirroring the .S field offsets (Asterinas: rip at offset 56)
decisions.perArch: x86 callee set = rbx/rbp/r12-r15 (+rsp,rip); aarch64 callee set = x19-x28 (+fp,lr,sp). Both skip FP/SIMD due to soft-float targets.

decisions.decision: Build the trap/interrupt path as macro/`global_asm!`-generated entry stubs that push a `#[repr(C)] TrapFrame` (caller-saved GPRs + vector/error-code) and call a single safe Rust dispatcher, then restore and return (iretq / eret). `install_trap_table` loads IDT (x86) / VBAR_EL1 (aarch64).
decisions.rationale: This is the OSTD `arch/x86/trap` model: a small asm shim per vector that normalises CPU state into a frame, then hands a safe value to Rust. The paper explicitly flags that architectural UBs (spoofed interrupts corrupting trap handlers, DMA, stack overflow) are beyond what safe Rust catches, so the trap shim is exactly the kind of code that must live in the audited tb-hal and present a sound frame to the safe layer. x86 needs 256 IDT vectors, distinguishing the ~8 vectors that push a hardware error code from those that do not (the stub equalises the frame). aarch64 needs the fixed 16-entry VBAR_EL1 table (4 exception classes x 4 originating states), each slot 128 bytes, table 2 KB-aligned.
assemblyUnits: x86_64 trap_entry.S: 256 vector stubs (macro), dummy-error-code push for non-error vectors, save caller-saved GPRs, call rust trap dispatch, restore, iretq; build+load IDT
aarch64 vectors.S: 16-entry VBAR_EL1 table at `.align 11`, each entry saves GPRs to TrapFrame, branches to rust dispatch, eret; msr VBAR_EL1
TrapFrame #[repr(C)] definitions per arch shared with the safe layer
decisions.perArch: x86: IDT + error-code asymmetry + (future) swapgs on privilege change. aarch64: single VBAR_EL1 table, exception syndrome read from ESR_EL1 in Rust, no error-code asymmetry.

decisions.decision: Ship v1 as single-vCPU per microVM (Mirage multikernel): build NO application-processor bring-up assembly. Explicitly do not write the x86 real-mode INIT-SIPI-SIPI AP trampoline nor an aarch64 PSCI CPU_ON secondary entry; per-CPU state collapses to a single static instance.
decisions.rationale: The framekernel paper's Invariant 8 ('A Task runs on at most one CPU at any given time'), enforced via a per-task is_running flag checked before context switch, is satisfied trivially when there is one vCPU, removing a class of SMP data-race UB from the TCB. The ARM64 boot doc's secondary-CPU machinery (spin-table / PSCI CPU_ON, cpu-release-addr, secondary x0-x3=0) is therefore out of scope for v1. This deletes the single largest and most error-prone asm asset in other kernels: Theseus's ap_realmode.asm is ~14.8 KB of 16-bit->64-bit AP trampoline; TABOS writes none of it.
assemblyUnits: DELIBERATELY ABSENT (tracked as 'not built' so agents do not add them): x86 ap_realmode/ap_boot trampoline; aarch64 PSCI CPU_ON secondary _start; multi-CPU GDT/IDT/TSS arrays
Per-CPU base setup stays (GS base / TPIDR_EL1) but initialises exactly one instance
decisions.perArch: x86: skip APIC AP startup + 16-bit trampoline. aarch64: skip PSCI CPU_ON path; keep a single PSCI SYSTEM_OFF/RESET wrapper for shutdown. riscv64 (future) would defer SBI HSM hart-start similarly.

decisions.decision: Target two tuples derived from the upstream Tier-2 bare-metal targets `x86_64-unknown-none` and `aarch64-unknown-none-softfloat`, materialised as pinned custom JSON specs (via `rustc -Z unstable-options --print target-spec-json`) so the linker script (PVH note + load address on x86; Image header + load address on aarch64), `relocation-model: static`, code model, and atomics width are frozen. Set `panic = "abort"` (Cargo profile + spec), keep red zone disabled and SSE/NEON off, and pin the exact rustc/Ferrocene toolchain version.
decisions.rationale: The embedonomicon prescribes exactly this workflow: copy a built-in spec via --print target-spec-json, set `panic-strategy: abort` (else you must provide eh_personality), choose atomics, and build with `-Z build-std`. The `x86_64-unknown-none` target already delivers the kernel-correct defaults we need: 'does not use any vector or floating-point registers', 'kernel code model by default', and System V 'without red zones' — so the x86 spec only overrides linker/layout. Custom JSON (vs using the built-ins raw) freezes these against upstream drift, which matters for a frozen-capability kernel. Qualification scope is asymmetric: Ferrocene currently qualifies `aarch64-unknown-none` (Armv8-A bare-metal, hard-float, cross-compiled from x86-64 Linux) but lists NO x86_64 bare-metal target — only `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` — so an aarch64 safety-qualification path exists while x86_64-none does not, a gap the spec must record.
assemblyUnits: Linker scripts (not asm, but they place the asm): x86_64 link.ld puts the PVH `.note.Xen` + entry and sets the load/phys base; aarch64 link.ld puts the 64-byte Image header first and sets the 2 MB-aligned base
Cargo `.cargo/config.toml` build-std=core,alloc + per-target rustflags pinning
decisions.perArch: x86_64-unknown-none: red zone off, SSE/AVX off, kernel code model already default. aarch64-unknown-none-softfloat: soft-float removes d8-d15 from switch; if safety qualification is later required, migrate aarch64 to Ferrocene-qualified `aarch64-unknown-none` (hard-float) with FP codegen disabled.

hardFacts.fact: Rust 1.88.0 (released 2025-06-26) stabilized naked functions: `#[unsafe(naked)]` attribute + a body of a single `core::arch::naked_asm!` call, described as a more ergonomic alternative to a `global_asm!` block, targeted at OS/embedded use. This is the floor for TABOS's pinned stable toolchain if it wants naked_asm! instead of pure global_asm!.

Firecracker x86_64 PVH entry register contract (from setup_regs/configure_segments_and_sregs): rip = ELF PHYS32 entry, rbx = PVH_INFO_START (hvm_start_info ptr), rflags = 0x2, CR0 = PE|ET with paging OFF, CR4 = 0, flat 4 GB code(0xc09b)/data(0xc093) GDT — i.e. 32-bit protected mode, kernel must build its own page tables and enter long mode.

Firecracker x86_64 LinuxBoot alternative enters in 64-bit long mode: CR0.PE + EFER.LME|LMA set, CR3=PML4 with Firecracker-built identity tables mapping VA [0,1GB) via 512 x 2MB pages, CR4.PAE, rsp=rbp=BOOT_STACK_POINTER, rsi = ZERO_PAGE_START (boot_params). Fewer asm lines but couples to the legacy zero-page.

ARM64 Linux boot contract (primary CPU): x0 = physical address of dtb, x1=x2=x3=0; MMU OFF; all interrupts masked in PSTATE.DAIF; CPU in non-secure EL2 (recommended) or EL1; decompressed Image has a 64-byte header with magic 0x644d5241 ('ARM\x64'). Secondary CPUs use spin-table or PSCI CPU_ON (deferred under single-vCPU).

x86_64 System V callee-saved set for context switch = rbx, rbp, r12, r13, r14, r15 (+ rsp, rip), exactly as stored in Asterinas OSTD switch.S / TaskContext (rip at struct offset 56). The 128-byte red zone must be disabled in kernel code; `x86_64-unknown-none` confirms extern "C" is System V 'without red zones', uses the kernel code model, and emits no SSE/AVX by default.

AAPCS64 callee-saved (call-preserved) general-purpose registers = r19-r28; r29 = FP (frame pointer), r30 = LR (link register), plus SP; r18 is the platform register (else caller-saved). Under a soft-float kernel target the otherwise callee-saved d8-d15 are not live, so the aarch64 context switch saves x19-x28 + fp + lr + sp only.

Firecracker requires an UNCOMPRESSED kernel (x86_64: vmlinux ELF; aarch64: PE/flat Image), implements no guest power management (guest `reboot` gracefully shuts the microVM down), and exposes no PCI bus unless launched with `--enable-pci` (default transport is virtio-MMIO) — so v1 needs no ACPI/PCI/legacy-BIOS enumeration asm.

Toolchain/target availability: `x86_64-unknown-none` is an upstream Tier-2 built-in (FP off, kernel code model, no red zone, ELF). Ferrocene's qualified bare-metal targets include `aarch64-unknown-none` (Armv8-A, hard-float, qualified only when cross-compiled from x86-64 Linux glibc) and thumbv7em-none-eabi(hf), but list NO x86_64 bare-metal target (only x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, full std) — x86_64-none has no Ferrocene qualification path today.

Asterinas reports its memory-safety TCB at ~14.0% of the codebase because all unsafe is confined to OSTD; OSTD's unsafe/asm is physically partitioned under ostd/src/arch/{x86,riscv,loongarch} with x86 split into boot, cpu, task(context switch, switch.S via global_asm!), trap, mm, irq, timer, serial, io, power. Invariant 8: a Task runs on at most one CPU at any time (trivially held under single-vCPU).

decisions.decision: Classify inline asm and naked functions as OUTSIDE the Ferrocene normatively-qualified subset, and subject every asm unit to the Ferrocene Safety Manual section 9 unsafe-code discipline as a hard, tracked constraint: unsafety localized to a single module that does exactly one task, with assertions/pre/post/invariants in BOTH the asm wrapper and its callers, safety comments on both sides, code review of the WHOLE module by >=2 senior engineers (or a third-party review), and both unit and integration tests.
decisions.rationale: The Ferrocene qualification scope is rustc 1.98.0 'limited to the Rust language as described in the FLS, and according to the requirements and restrictions outlined in this safety manual.' But FLS Chapter 22 (Inline Assembly), which is the ONLY place asm!/global_asm!/naked_asm! are specified, opens with 'Note: The contents of this section are informational.' i.e. it is non-normative and carries no requirement traceability, unlike the rest of the language. So asm cannot lean on the tool qualification and instead inherits exactly the manual measures the Safety Manual prescribes for unsafe code (section 9 'Handling Unsafety' verbatim list). This converts the FLS gap into a concrete checklist the build agents can track per asm unit.
assemblyUnits: Per-arch foundation crate `arch/asm/` module that is the sole holder of asm! / global_asm! / naked_asm! (every other crate is #![forbid(unsafe_code)])
A SAFETY/contract doc-block template enforced on _start, the long-mode trampoline, context-switch, trap/exception entry, and early_putc
decisions.perArch: aarch64: Armv8-A bare-metal (hard-float) IS a Ferrocene-qualified TARGET, so aarch64 asm runs nearer the qualified envelope. x86_64: Ferrocene qualifies x86-64 only as a Linux glibc host/target, NOT bare-metal, so the x86_64 PVH foundation runs entirely outside even the host-qualified target set -> extra scrutiny weighting on x86_64 asm. riscv64: future, no Ferrocene target.

decisions.decision: Require a 3-part machine-checkable header on EVERY asm unit: (a) a pre/postcondition comment contract, (b) a register-clobber + ABI annotation that names the chosen clobber_abi and any explicit clobbers and restates the FLS hard rules it must uphold, and (c) the name of the paired test that exercises it.
decisions.rationale: seL4's proofs explicitly ASSUME the correctness of assembly code and boot code, and the seL4 Foundation states 'this is where manual validation and scrutiny can focus on' - so the unverifiable surface must be self-documenting and testable. Ferrocene section 9 already requires assertions/pre/post/invariants plus safety comments in unsafe code. FLS Chapter 22 gives the exact obligations the ABI annotation must encode: a register not declared as output MUST hold the same value on exit (22.1:4) else it is undefined behavior (22.1:72); the stack pointer must be restored on exit; on x86 the EFLAGS direction flag DF MUST be cleared on exit (22.4:9) else it is undefined behavior (22.4:10). The clobber_abi tables (22.5) enumerate exactly which registers 'C'/'sysv64'/'win64'/'aapcs' clobber, which is the canonical content of annotation (b).
assemblyUnits: Contract header instances on _start (x86_64+aarch64), the 32->64 long-mode trampoline (x86_64), context-switch save/restore, trap/exception entry, early_putc
A clippy/CI lint or doc-test that fails the build when an asm unit lacks the (a)(b)(c) header
decisions.perArch: x86_64: annotation must list the sysv64 clobber set (ax,cx,dx,si,di,r8-r11,xmm0-31,k0-7,mm0-7,st0-7,tmm0-7) and the DF-cleared-on-exit postcondition. aarch64: must list the aapcs/'C' clobber set (x0-17, x18 when not reserved, x30, v0-31, p0-15, ffr) and respect x18 (platform reg), x29 (frame ptr), x30 (lr) constraints.

decisions.decision: Boot x86_64 via PVH direct boot (emit the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY ELF note) and aarch64 via the Linux arm64 Image boot protocol; the only hand-written boot asm is a single `_start` naked function per arch carrying the documented entry-register contract.
decisions.rationale: Firecracker supports PVH direct boot for x86 kernels and documents it as 'simpler than the Linux boot mode.' The Xen PVH ABI fixes the entry state: 32-bit protected mode, CR0 bit0 (PE) set with all other writable bits clear, paging off, CR4 all-clear, flat 0..0xFFFFFFFF segments, and ebx = physical address of the hvm_start_info struct, with 'The OS is in charge of setting up its own stack, GDT and IDT' (start_info.rsdp_paddr gives the ACPI RSDP). The Linux arm64 protocol fixes the aarch64 entry: x0 = physical address of the DTB, x1-x3 = 0, all interrupts masked in PSTATE.DAIF, non-secure EL1/EL2, MMU off, 64-byte Image header magic 0x644d5241. Naked functions are now stable (Rust 1.88.0, 2025-06-26: #[unsafe(naked)] + core::arch::naked_asm!), so the boot stub is a naked fn living INSIDE the audited foundation crate rather than an external .S file.
assemblyUnits: x86_64 `_start` naked fn: build a 64-bit GDT, build identity-mapped PML4/PDPT/PD, set CR4.PAE, set EFER.LME, load CR3, set CR0.PG|PE, far-jump into the 64-bit code segment, clear BSS, load RSP, call rust_main(hvm_start_info ptr from ebx)
aarch64 `_start` naked fn: load SP, set VBAR_EL1 to the exception-vector table, clear BSS, (optional) program TTBR0/TCR/SCTLR to enable MMU, call rust_main(x0=dtb)
ELF PHYS32_ENTRY note section (x86_64) and the 64-byte arm64 Image header (aarch64) emitted via global_asm!/linker script
decisions.perArch: x86_64 performs a 32-bit->64-bit mode transition inside _start (paging+long mode must be enabled by the kernel); aarch64 is already 64-bit at EL1 with no mode switch but MMU is off. riscv64 (future): SBI hands off with a0=hartid, a1=dtb in S-mode - note only.

decisions.decision: Define a two-phase early-boot model and FORBID TigerStyle assert!() until Phase 1; Phase 0 (from `_start` until SP is loaded and BSS cleared) may only spin (hlt/wfe) on fault and optionally poke a single raw byte to the UART. Phase 1 (SP+BSS+UART-writer+#[panic_handler] established) is the earliest point assertions and serial-debug become available.
decisions.rationale: TigerStyle mandates a minimum of two assertions per function and asserting all arguments/preconditions, but assert!() formats a message and drives the panic machinery, which needs a stack, a panic handler, and an output sink - none of which exist at the architectural entry point (PVH spec: 'the OS is in charge of setting up its own stack'; arm64: SP is undefined on entry). Therefore the discipline must be staged: Phase 0 has no stack so it can carry no assertions and no formatting and must route any fault to a minimal halt loop; Phase 1 begins the instant SP, cleared BSS, a UART writer, and a #[panic_handler] exist, and from there TigerStyle density and serial debug apply. Firecracker exposes a legacy serial console (16550-class) plus an i8042 reset device, giving exactly one pre-stack debug primitive: a single-byte UART poke.
assemblyUnits: `early_putc` leaf: write one byte to the platform UART usable before a stack exists (x86_64 COM1 I/O port via out; aarch64 PL011 data register via MMIO store)
The SP-load + BSS-zeroing unit whose completion is the Phase-0 -> Phase-1 boundary
`hang`/panic-spin units: x86_64 `cli; hlt` loop, aarch64 `msr daifset,#0xf; wfe` loop
decisions.perArch: x86_64: pre-stack debug is an `out` to the 16550 COM1 port; halt is cli;hlt. aarch64: pre-stack debug is an MMIO store to the PL011 DR; halt is DAIF-mask + wfe. Both must run before the kernel enables paging/MMU so they use physical addresses.

decisions.decision: Track unsafe-and-asm as a hard CI budget metric: assert #![forbid(unsafe_code)] on every non-foundation crate, count unsafe blocks and asm statement-lines in the foundation crate against a committed budget file, confine 100% of unsafe+asm to that one crate, and fail the build on any increase without two-reviewer sign-off.
decisions.rationale: Asterinas' framekernel architecture confines ALL unsafe Rust to a small auditable framework (OSTD) with the rest in safe Rust, explicitly so that 'the memory safety of the kernel can be reduced to that of the OS Framework, thus minimizing the Trusted Computing Base', and lists 'Minimalism - the framework should be kept as small as possible. No functionality should be implemented inside the framework if doing it outside is possible' as a core requirement. Ferrocene constraint RUSTC_CSTR_0100_UNSAFETY plus section 9 require identifying and evaluating EVERY instance of unsafe code. Making both counts a committed, diffable number turns the framekernel TCB into an enforced gate, keeping the <=15 kSLOC kernel's trusted surface visibly bounded and giving the multi-agent build an objective regression signal.
assemblyUnits: No new asm, but the budget enumerates the trusted asm inventory to count: _start x2 arch, x86_64 long-mode trampoline, context-switch, trap entry, early_putc, plus any barrier/atomic/MSR-access intrinsics
A CI gate script (counts `unsafe {`, asm! / global_asm! / naked_asm! statements) comparing against budget.toml
decisions.perArch: Counts are maintained per-arch because the asm inventory is arch-split; x86_64 carries the extra long-mode trampoline that aarch64 does not.

decisions.decision: Pin a single rustc and make the whole build (including asm) bit-reproducible via a Nix flake; keep ALL asm as in-language naked_asm!/global_asm! compiled by that pinned rustc (no external .S, no separate assembler to pin), and verify the per-arch linker scripts under the Ferrocene build constraints.
decisions.rationale: Reproducibility is what lets an auditor re-derive the trusted binary, and the Ferrocene Safety Manual already pins the build envelope: RUSTC_CSTR_0030 (inspect build logs / confirm correct tool versions and options), RUSTC_CSTR_0040 (clean the build environment of former artifacts), RUSTC_CSTR_0020 + the Degraded-Environment list (clean disallowed env vars), RUSTC_CSTR_0050 (treat warnings as errors on the final build), and RUSTC_CSTR_0120 (ensure linker scripts are interpreted correctly by rust-lld). Ferrocene qualifies exactly one compiler version (rustc 1.98.0 in current scope), so TABOS pins one rustc in a Nix flake; emitting asm as in-language global_asm!/naked_asm! means it is produced by that same pinned compiler, eliminating a second toolchain from the trusted base and keeping the asm inside the audited crate. Asterinas demonstrates the pattern with a Nix-based reproducible build/test image.
assemblyUnits: A checked-in linker script per arch (x86_64 places the PVH ELF note + identity sections; aarch64 emits the Image header + text_offset layout), each verified per RUSTC_CSTR_0120 against rust-lld
All global_asm!/naked_asm! blocks built only by the pinned rustc
Nix flake pinning rustc + rust-lld + target.json
decisions.perArch: Separate target spec + linker script per arch: x86_64 needs the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY note section and a 2-stage (32/64-bit) layout; aarch64 needs the 64-byte Image header and 2MB-aligned text_offset.

decisions.decision: Encode the layout/ABI facts the asm depends on as compile-time const assertions (struct offsets, GDT/IDT descriptor values, page-table bit positions, UART register offsets) so drift breaks COMPILATION not boot; from Phase 1 on, apply TigerStyle runtime assertion density and crash-on-violation, with paired asserts straddling each asm FFI boundary.
decisions.rationale: TigerStyle: 'Assertions detect programmer errors... the only correct way to handle corrupt code is to crash'; minimum two assertions per function; pair assertions across two different code paths; assert the positive space you expect AND the negative space you do not; and 'Assert the relationships of compile-time constants... Compile-time assertions are extremely powerful because they are able to check a program's design integrity before the program even executes.' The asm units read/write fields at fixed offsets (context-switch touches a saved-register struct; _start writes GDT/IDT/page-table entries) - encoding each offset/bit as a const { assert!(...) } makes a layout change a build error rather than a triple-fault. Wrapping each asm! call site with an input assertion before and an output assertion after satisfies the pair-assertion rule on the most dangerous boundary.
assemblyUnits: const-asserted offset table consumed by the context-switch save/restore asm
const-asserted GDT/IDT/page-table-entry constant blocks consumed by x86_64 _start
Pre-asm input asserts and post-asm output asserts wrapping every asm! invocation in Phase-1 code
decisions.perArch: x86_64: const-assert GDT entry encodings, PML4/PDPT/PD entry bits, IDT gate layout. aarch64: const-assert VBAR_EL1 alignment, translation-table descriptor bits, PSTATE/DAIF mask values.

decisions.decision: Gate every asm unit on a named paired test that executes it in BOTH the real VMM (Firecracker) and QEMU per arch in CI, asserting a Phase-1 serial marker; because v1 is single-vCPU (Mirage one-vCPU-per-microVM), no AP-bringup asm or test ships in v1 and the PSCI CPU_ON / PVH VCPUOP_up paths are explicitly deferred and tracked.
decisions.rationale: seL4 leaves assembly and boot code to 'manual validation and scrutiny' because the proof cannot reach them; TABOS substitutes executable tests as that validation. Ferrocene section 9 requires unit AND integration testing of unsafe code, and constraint RUSTC_CSTR_0070_TEST requires integration testing 'on the final application... using build protocols as close as possible to those used for the final build.' Firecracker is the real target VMM, so the boot-path asm (_start, trampoline) is validated by actually booting under Firecracker plus a QEMU microvm/virt smoke test, with a Phase-1 serial byte as the pass condition; leaf asm (context-switch, trap entry, early_putc) gets focused QEMU integration tests. Starting single-vCPU (a fixed TABOS decision) means AP startup asm is simply absent in v1, so its tests are scoped out rather than stubbed.
assemblyUnits: CI harness that launches Firecracker with a minimal microVM config + the TABOS kernel and waits for the serial marker
QEMU smoke target: `-machine microvm` (x86_64) and `-machine virt` (aarch64), single vCPU
A serial-marker emit at the Phase-0->Phase-1 boundary used as the universal boot-pass assertion
decisions.perArch: x86_64: boot PVH image under Firecracker and QEMU microvm. aarch64: boot Image under Firecracker and QEMU virt. Both single-vCPU. Deferred (tracked): x86_64 PVH VCPUOP_up and aarch64 PSCI CPU_ON (ARM DEN 0022A) AP-bringup asm + tests.

hardFacts.fact: FLS Chapter 22 'Inline Assembly' (the only specification of asm!/global_asm!/naked_asm!) is explicitly marked 'Note: The contents of this section are informational' - i.e. non-normative. This is the load-bearing basis for treating asm as outside the normatively-qualified subset; the inference 'informational FLS section => excluded from the qualified-subset verification' must be adversarially confirmed against the Ferrocene traceability-matrix/qualification-report, since the Safety Manual does not contain an explicit 'inline assembly is excluded' constraint.

Ferrocene current qualified scope = rustc 1.98.0, qualified targets include Armv8-A bare-metal (hard-float) and Armv7E-M, but x86-64 is qualified ONLY as x86-64 Linux glibc host/target (no bare-metal x86-64). Verify exact version and target list for the specific Ferrocene release TABOS pins, as scope changes per release.

Naked functions were stabilized in Rust 1.88.0 (2025-06-26) as #[unsafe(naked)] + core::arch::naked_asm!; inline asm! and global_asm! were stabilized in Rust 1.59.0 (2022-02-24) for x86, x86-64, ARM, AArch64, and RISC-V. The pinned toolchain must therefore be >= 1.88 to use naked fns for _start.

x86_64 PVH entry state (Xen PVH ABI, used by Firecracker via XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY): 32-bit protected mode, CR0.PE=1 with all other writable bits clear, paging OFF, CR4=0, ebx = physical address of hvm_start_info, flat 0..0xFFFFFFFF code/data segments, OS must build its own stack/GDT/IDT; start_info.rsdp_paddr locates ACPI RSDP. Confirm Firecracker actually selects PVH (not Linux 64-bit boot) for the ELF kernel image type TABOS ships.

aarch64 entry state (Linux arm64 boot protocol): primary CPU x0 = physical address of DTB, x1=x2=x3=0; all interrupts masked in PSTATE.DAIF; non-secure EL2 (recommended) or EL1; MMU off; 64-byte Image header with magic 0x644d5241 ('ARM\x64'); secondary CPUs brought up via PSCI CPU_ON (ARM DEN 0022A). Verify the exact EL Firecracker enters the guest at (expected EL1) and that it passes a DTB rather than ACPI on aarch64.

FLS undefined-behavior rules the asm ABI annotations must encode: a register not declared as an output register must hold its entry value on exit (22.1:4) else it is UB (22.1:72); the stack pointer must be restored on exit (sp is an unsupported explicit operand for this reason); on x86 the EFLAGS direction flag DF must be cleared on exit (22.4:9) else UB (22.4:10). clobber_abi register sets per arch are tabulated in 22.5 (sysv64/win64/aapcs/etc.).

seL4's machine-checked proofs assume (do NOT verify) the correctness of: assembly code, boot code, the machine/hardware interface, hardware behavior, and DMA - and the seL4 Foundation states these are 'where manual validation and scrutiny can focus on.' This is the precedent for TABOS substituting documented contracts + executable tests for the unverifiable asm/boot surface.

Firecracker's machine model exposes a serial console (16550-class) and an i8042 partial keyboard controller (used only to signal guest-requested reboot); clocksources are x86_64 tsc/kvm-clock and aarch64 arch_sys_counter. The exact early-debug UART addresses used by early_putc (x86_64 COM1 I/O port, conventionally 0x3F8; aarch64 PL011 MMIO base) are NOT given in design.md and must be confirmed against Firecracker's arch device layout/source before hardcoding them in Phase-0 asm.

decisions.decision: Split testing into two tiers: (a) hosted `cargo test` on the host triple for every `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` safe-Rust layer (capability algebra, page-table index/permission math, scheduler bookkeeping), and (b) an on-target custom runner that boots the real no_std image in a VM for everything touching asm/MMIO/privileged state. Foundation asm is validated ONLY by tier (b); it is never linked into a host cargo test.
decisions.rationale: phil-opp shows the std `test` crate cannot link in no_std (`error[E0463]: can't find crate for test`, because `test` depends on libstd), so kernel-internal code needs a bare-metal harness; meanwhile the framekernel pattern guarantees the upper layers contain zero asm and are pure logic, so they are sound (and ~100x faster) as ordinary host unit tests. This matches Firecracker's own split: pytest for VM-level behaviour plus native-Rust integration tests under `cargo test`.
assemblyUnits: (none new) — this decision DEFINES that all foundation asm units listed under D3/D5/D6/D9 are gated exclusively by the on-target runner, never by host cargo test
decisions.perArch: Host tests are arch-neutral (run on the CI box's native triple). The on-target tier is per-arch, built for x86_64-unknown-none and aarch64-unknown-none custom targets.

decisions.decision: Build the on-target runner on the phil-opp pattern (Testable trait, per-test serial `name ... [ok]` line, runner writes pass/fail to the platform exit device then halts) BUT register tests via a stable linker-section distributed slice (linkme-style `link_section` + `#[used]`), NOT the nightly `custom_test_frameworks` feature, so the test image builds on the same pinned Ferrocene/stable toolchain as the shipped kernel.
decisions.rationale: phil-opp's harness relies on `#![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]`, `#[test_case]`, and `reexport_test_harness_main` — all nightly-only — and the x86-interrupt ABoI it uses is likewise experimental (`issue #40180`). TABOS targets a Ferrocene-qualified stable toolchain, so a nightly feature in the build path is disqualifying; a linker-section registry reproduces the same collect-and-run shape on stable. Serial is chosen as the result channel because it is the one output portable across both QEMU and Firecracker (phil-opp drives a 16550 UART at COM1 0x3F8).
assemblyUnits: serial putchar primitive: write one byte to the platform UART (poll TX-ready, store byte)
platform exit/halt primitive invoked by the runner after the last test
decisions.perArch: Collection mechanism is arch-neutral. The serial putchar and exit primitives diverge per arch (see D4): x86 16550 port I/O vs aarch64 PL011-class MMIO.

decisions.decision: Make the boot bring-up test the executable Definition-of-Done for the per-arch `_start`: a CI job builds the no_std image, boots it under QEMU `microvm` (x86_64) / QEMU `virt` (aarch64), and asserts a serial line `hello from rust_main` plus a fixed 64-bit magic marker, followed by a clean VM exit inside a hard timeout. Firecracker boot is a SECONDARY smoke test on `.metal` runners using a dev/debug Firecracker build with the serial console enabled.
decisions.rationale: QEMU `microvm` is explicitly 'inspired by Firecracker and constructed after its machine model' (no PCI, no ACPI, ISA serial + virtio-mmio), so it reproduces the Firecracker machine on any CI box without KVM or a bare-metal `.metal` host. Firecracker is the secondary (not primary) gate because production Firecracker builds 'do not expose the serial console port' — the serial-marker DoD therefore requires a dev build of Firecracker, whereas QEMU always exposes ttyS0. The marker proves `_start` set the stack, cleared BSS, and reached `rust_main`.
assemblyUnits: `_start` entry trampoline: set SP to the top of the boot stack, zero the BSS, (x86) reload a flat GDT / confirm long mode, (aarch64) confirm EL1 + stash the FDT pointer from x0, then call `rust_main`
serial putchar (reused) to emit the marker
decisions.perArch: x86_64: QEMU `-M microvm` (this machine type is x86-only). aarch64: QEMU `-M virt`. Both arches additionally bootable under Firecracker. riscv64: future, not gated.

decisions.decision: Standardize a per-arch deterministic VM-exit channel so tests fail-closed with an exit STATUS, not only by serial scraping, and bound every on-target test with a hard wall-clock timeout (set 60 s; phil-opp default is 300 s). x86_64 under QEMU uses `isa-debug-exit` (out to port 0xf4); under Firecracker it triggers a deliberate CPU reset; aarch64 uses PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF`/`SYSTEM_RESET` via `hvc`, with QEMU semihosting `SYS_EXIT` as the QEMU-virt fallback.
decisions.rationale: phil-opp: cargo treats any nonzero process exit as failure, so the runner maps `isa-debug-exit` success 0x10 to host exit 33 then to 0; and endless boot loops (bad load, or an uncaught exception that triple-faults and resets) MUST be bounded by a timeout or they hang CI forever. Firecracker has no isa-debug-exit, but its actions doc states plainly 'Firecracker exits on CPU reset' (i8042/SendCtrlAltDel or a triple fault), giving an equivalent termination signal. QEMU microvm doc confirms a guest-initiated triple-fault plus host `-no-reboot` makes QEMU 'terminate its own execution gracefully'.
assemblyUnits: x86_64: port-out exit primitive (`out 0xf4, eax`) for QEMU isa-debug-exit
x86_64: deliberate-reset primitive for Firecracker termination (triple fault / i8042 pulse)
aarch64: PSCI call primitive (`hvc #0` with function id SYSTEM_OFF/SYSTEM_RESET)
halt-loop primitive after exit (`hlt`/`wfi` in an infinite loop, since the exit device's effect is not visible to the compiler)
decisions.perArch: x86_64 isa-debug-exit (QEMU) vs CPU-reset (Firecracker); aarch64 PSCI hvc (Firecracker + QEMU virt) or semihosting (QEMU). riscv64 future: SBI SRST extension.

decisions.decision: Gate the context-switch asm with a canary test: spawn two kernel tasks that yield to each other at least 1000 times; assert (a) deterministic A,B,A,B... alternation via a shared counter, AND (b) that a unique sentinel pre-loaded by task A into EVERY ABI callee-saved register is bit-for-bit intact after control returns through `context_switch`. Either failure is a hard fail.
decisions.rationale: The context-switch asm's entire contract is 'save the callee-saved set into prev, restore from next, swap stacks and PC'. The exact set is ABI-fixed and falsifiable: x86_64 System V callee-saved = rbx, rbp, rsp, r12, r13, r14, r15 (phil-opp's preserved-register table, from the System V psABI); AArch64 AAPCS64 callee-saved = r19-r28 plus r29(FP)/r30(LR) and SP, plus the low 64 bits of v8-v15. The canary is the only direct proof the asm saved/restored exactly that set and nothing leaked.
assemblyUnits: `context_switch(prev: *mut Ctx, next: *const Ctx)` as a `#[unsafe(naked)]` function: push callee-saved regs + SP + return address into prev, pop them from next, return into next
first-switch task entry trampoline that establishes an initial fake frame so a brand-new task can be `context_switch`-ed into
decisions.perArch: x86_64 saves rbx,rbp,r12-r15 + rsp + rip(via the pushed return address). aarch64 saves x19-x30 + sp (and optionally d8-d15 low halves for FP tasks). riscv64 future: s0-s11, ra, sp.

decisions.decision: Gate the trap path with three on-target assertions per arch: (a) execute a breakpoint (`int3` / `brk #0`) and assert the handler ran AND execution resumed; (b) deliberately fault (write to an unmapped address / `udf`) and assert the handler decoded the correct cause; (c) provoke a kernel stack overflow and assert it is caught on a dedicated fault stack instead of triple-faulting. The handler must surface the correct fault class to a test-observable location.
decisions.rationale: phil-opp's breakpoint test (`int3` -> handler -> resume) and stack-overflow test (endless recursion -> guard-page page fault -> double fault taken on an IST stack, asserted via exit_qemu success) transpose directly. Fault decode is per-arch and falsifiable: x86_64 uses the IDT vector number + pushed error code + CR2 (page-fault address); aarch64 uses ESR_EL1's EC (exception-class) field + FAR_EL1 (faulting VA). The stack-overflow case requires a known-good fault stack — x86 an IST entry in the 64-bit TSS, aarch64 a dedicated SP_EL1 handler stack — exactly the 'switch to a predefined known-good stack at hardware level' that phil-opp's double-fault post sets up.
assemblyUnits: x86_64: build the 16-byte-entry IDT + `lidt`; set up TSS with an IST entry + `ltr`; per-vector naked trap stubs that save the GPR frame and call the extern "C" Rust handler, returning via `iretq`

aarch64: a 2KB-aligned, 16-entry exception vector table (0x80 byte stride) installed via `msr VBAR_EL1`; per-entry naked trampoline that saves x0-x30 + SP + ELR_EL1 + SPSR_EL1, reads ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1, calls the Rust handler, then `eret`
decisions.perArch: x86_64 COULD use nightly `extern "x86-interrupt"` (phil-opp), but TABOS uses `#[unsafe(naked)]` stubs (stable since 1.88) on BOTH arches, because aarch64 has no interrupt calling convention and Ferrocene forbids nightly features. riscv64 future: `stvec`, `scause`/`stval`.

decisions.decision: Run the entire on-target suite (boot, context-switch, trap) as a CI matrix across {x86_64, aarch64} x {QEMU primary, Firecracker secondary}, gating merges on BOTH arches. Any foundation function with a `#[cfg(target_arch)]` split must carry a test that executes on each arch it compiles for.
decisions.rationale: The asm is the most arch-divergent code in the kernel; a green x86_64 run says nothing about the aarch64 vector table or PSCI exit. Firecracker supports exactly x86_64 and aarch64 Linux (getting-started / kernel-policy), which is precisely the v1 target set, so the matrix is two columns wide and no broader. riscv64 is noted but explicitly NOT gated in v1.
assemblyUnits: (none new) — executes the D3/D5/D6 asm units on each arch under each backend
decisions.perArch: Identical test source, two arches, two VMM backends; QEMU microvm for x86_64 and QEMU virt for aarch64, Firecracker for both on .metal runners.

decisions.decision: Scope formal verification to the SAFE Rust only: apply Kani (bounded model checking) and/or Verus proof harnesses to page-table index arithmetic, address-translation round-trips, capability-derivation monotonicity, and bitflag well-formedness; explicitly mark all naked asm, `_start`, the trap stubs, and `context_switch` as OUT of formal scope and covered empirically by D5/D6. Record this 'asm is tested, not proven' boundary in the framekernel audit document.
decisions.rationale: Kani 'uses model checking to analyze Rust programs' and checks undefined behavior + correctness properties, but it reasons about Rust, not raw inline/naked assembly or hardware side effects (its documented limitations exclude features like concurrency); so the trust story for asm is empirical, never formal. Making the boundary explicit prevents the audit from mistaking a verified safe layer for a verified unsafe foundation — the foundation's correctness rests on the on-target tests above.
assemblyUnits: (none) — this decision defines the NEGATIVE space: every asm unit in D3/D5/D6 is explicitly excluded from proof obligations and assigned to test coverage instead
decisions.perArch: Arch-neutral: the proven page-table/capability logic is shared safe Rust; the per-arch asm it excludes (vector tables, context switch) is the same set enumerated per arch above.

decisions.decision: Build every on-target test as its own bare `#![no_std] #![no_main]` binary (one DoD per test, phil-opp integration-test style) against checked-in custom target JSONs (`x86_64-tabos-none.json`, `aarch64-tabos-none.json`) using the pinned Ferrocene/stable toolchain, `-Z build-std=core,alloc`, and the SAME linker script as the production image. Tests that cannot continue past the event under test (stack overflow, double fault, should-panic) declare `harness = false` and run single-shot.
decisions.rationale: phil-opp: integration tests are 'completely separate executables', each defining its own `_start` and panic handler, and the stack-overflow / should-panic tests set `harness = false` precisely because execution cannot continue after the fault. Building each test with the identical target spec + linker script means it exercises the REAL `_start`, relocation, and section layout, not a host approximation — closing the gap between what CI proves and what ships.
assemblyUnits: per-test `_start` trampoline that shares the production `_start` path then jumps into the single test body
test panic handler that emits serial `[failed]` + the panic info, then invokes the D4 exit primitive
decisions.perArch: Two target JSONs (x86_64 / aarch64), one suite; same linker script per arch as the shippable kernel.

hardFacts.fact: AArch64 AAPCS64 callee-saved set (the registers the context-switch canary test must check): r19-r28 are callee-saved general-purpose registers; r29(FP), r30(LR) and SP are callee-saved with special roles; SIMD/FP v8-v15 are callee-saved but only their bottom 64 bits ('Registers r19-r29 and SP are Callee-saved... Registers v8-v15 are Callee-saved and the remaining registers (v0-v7, v16-v31) are Caller-saved').
x86_64 System V callee-saved (preserved) registers: rbx, rbp, rsp, r12, r13, r14, r15; scratch (caller-saved): rax, rcx, rdx, rsi, rdi, r8, r9, r10, r11.

Naked functions are stable as of Rust 1.88.0 (released 2025-06-26): `#[unsafe(naked)]` attribute + a single `core::arch::naked_asm!` body, 'used in low-level settings like Rust's compiler-builtins, operating systems'. By contrast `custom_test_frameworks` and the `abi_x86_interrupt` calling convention remain nightly-only (x86-interrupt is tracked under issue #40180).

QEMU isa-debug-exit semantics: writing value v to the iobase port (phil-opp uses iobase=0xf4, iosize=0x04) makes QEMU exit with status (v<<1)|1; the runner uses success code 0x10 -> exit 33 -> bootimage remaps to host 0. The `microvm` machine has no PCI and no ACPI, provides an ISA bus + one (optional) ISA 16550 serial + up to 8 virtio-mmio devices, and is 'inspired by Firecracker and constructed after its machine model'; it is x86-only.

Firecracker termination + serial constraints: 'Firecracker exits on CPU reset', and a guest reset can be driven via the emulated i8042 (SendCtrlAltDel) — Firecracker has NO isa-debug-exit device. Critically, 'In production builds, Firecracker does not expose the serial console port', so the serial-marker boot DoD requires a dev/debug Firecracker build. Firecracker supports exactly x86_64 and aarch64 Linux.

x86 trap-test facts: breakpoint is vector 3 (`int3`), double fault is vector 8; each IDT entry is 16 bytes; the IST is 7 stack pointers held in the 64-bit TSS and selected per-handler via IDT options bits 0-2; a kernel stack overflow hitting the guard page escalates page-fault -> double-fault -> triple-fault (reset) unless the double-fault handler is given a separate IST stack.

aarch64 trap-test decode facts ASSERTED FROM DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE, NOT YET FETCHED FROM AN AUTHORITATIVE ARM SOURCE THIS PASS — MUST be verified before shipping: synchronous-exception cause is in ESR_EL1 (EC = exception-class field), faulting address in FAR_EL1; the exception vector base is VBAR_EL1, a 2KB-aligned table of 16 entries at 0x80-byte stride; `brk` raises a breakpoint and `udf` an undefined-instruction trap. Verify against ARM ARM (DDI 0487) — wiki.osdev.org/AArch64_Exception_Handling was Cloudflare-blocked (HTTP 403).
hardFacts.source: UNVERIFIED — fetch ARM Architecture Reference Manual DDI 0487 (developer.arm.com) and Firecracker aarch64 vCPU setup source to confirm ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1/VBAR_EL1 layout

hardFacts.fact: Per-arch serial device for the boot marker: x86_64 Firecracker/QEMU console is ttyS0 = 16550 COM1 at port 0x3F8 (confirmed: Firecracker getting-started uses `console=ttyS0`; phil-opp drives 0x3F8). aarch64 Firecracker exposes a serial console (PL011-class MMIO UART) — the PL011 identity and its MMIO base were NOT confirmed from source this pass and MUST be verified.
